sn45.8:1.2: See note on SN 12.2:1.2 for a discussion of “analysis” discourses. | The same definitions are found at DN 22:21.1 and MN 141:24.1.
sn45.8:3.1: “View” (diṭṭhi, Sanskrit dṛṣṭi) is “seeing” and hence a term for “understanding” (Aitareya Upaniṣad 3.2). In Buddhism, it means a particular way of seeing or perspective, a theory or view through which one makes sense of the world. By itself it is neutral, although “views” in plural sometimes stands for the thicket of wrong views.
sn45.8:3.2: The fourth noble truth is the path, while the first path factor is the noble truths. These two teachings are different perspectives on the same dhamma. | Ignorance is described in the opposite way (MN 9:64–66.7, SN 12.2:14.2).
sn45.8:4.1: Saṅkappa is normally a synonym of vitakka in the suttas, hence it may be rendered “thought” (SN 14.8:3.3, MN 60:24.2). It is, however, not just verbalized thought, but the direction in which one applies the mind, hence “intention” (MN 41:14.3), “resolve”, or “purpose”. It has a directionality to it: in Rig Veda 10.164.5 it is the “evil intent” wished upon those who we hate, whereas in Atharva Veda 3.25.2 it is a quality of the arrow of love. For this reason, I usually render it as “purpose”. This factor is the emotional counterpart of right view, ensuring that the path is motivated by love and compassion.
sn45.8:4.2: Abyāpāda is “non-ill-will” or simply “good will”.
sn45.8:5.1: The factors of speech and action are defined in detail under the gradual training at MN 27:13.4, and under the ten pathways of skillful deeds at MN 41:12.4.
sn45.8:6.2: The first three of the five precepts. | We would expect “sexual misconduct”, which is the betrayal of trust or otherwise causing harm in a sexual relationship (MN 141:27.2. However, the text has abrahmacariyā (“unchastity”), which normally applies to renunciates only (MN 96:13.1, AN 3.121:1.5). Bodhi (Connected Discourses) emends under the assumption that the SN reading is probably a scribal error introduced after the time of the commentary, which is silent. The parallel at SA 784 has 婬 (“fornication”) which literally supports abrahmacariyā, but apparently can be used for both versions. “Sexual misconduct” and “unchastity” both work here; it is just a matter of the audience. Since there is no strong reason to emend, I translate according to the text.
sn45.8:7.2: Wrong livelihood is defined at MN 117:29.2 and AN 5.177:1.3, and in detail for renunciates at DN 2:56.1.
sn45.8:8.1: Expanded to a full saṁyutta at SN 49.
sn45.8:10.1: “Immersion” (samādhi) is treated in SN 53 and throughout the Suttas, eg. SN 12.23:2.15, MN 44:12.2 and notes. The primary sense is the mind “collected” or “immersed” inside itself, with a secondary connotation of radiant illumination. | Before the Buddha, we find samādhi and related terms in the sense of gathering kindling for the fire. The past participle samāhita occurs in the sense “collected, contained”, as the essence of waters are “contained” in the Sun (Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 5.1.2.7). Certain Upaniṣadic usages presage the Buddhist sense, though expressed in a mystical form designed to be as obscure as possible. A realized sage, being “collected”, sees only the self in the self and is free from evil, having become one with Divinity (Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 4.4.24). For such a one, the whole world is “contained” in the space within the heart—earth and sky, fire and wind, sun and moon, all beings and all desires (Chāndogya Upaniṣad 8.1.3–5). The hidden Self, finer than the finest, larger than the largest, may only be discerned by one who is peaceful and “collected” (Kaṭha Upaniṣad 2.24).
sn45.8:10.2: Jhāna is a state of “elevated consciousness” (adhicitta), so all the terms have an elevated sense. | The plural form indicates that “sensual pleasures” includes sense experience, which the meditator can turn away from since they no longer have any desire for it. | The “unskillful qualities” are the five hindrances. | The “rapture and bliss born of seclusion” is the happiness of abandoning the hindrances and freedom from sense impingement. | “Placing the mind and keeping it connected” (vitakka, vicāra) uses terms that mean “thought” in coarse consciousness, but which in “elevated consciousness” refer to the subtle function of applying the mind to the meditation.
sn45.8:10.3: Each jhāna begins as the least refined aspect of the previous jhāna ends. This is not consciously directed, but describes the natural process of settling. The meditator is now fully confident and no longer needs to apply their mind: it is simply still and fully unified.
sn45.8:10.4: The emotional response to bliss matures from the subtle thrill of rapture to the poise of equanimity. Mindfulness is present in all states of deep meditation, but with equanimity it becomes prominent.
sn45.8:10.5: The emotional poise of equanimity leads to the feeling of pleasure settling into the more subtle neutral feeling. Pain and sadness have been abandoned long before, but are emphasized here as they are subtle counterpart of pleasure.
